subtitle: Product Engineering Delivery · Atlassian · Agile Coaching
hero_title:
The SM who owns
the Jira, the metrics,
and the outcome.
hero_sub: Ten years running Agile delivery across product engineering teams building commercial software. Deep Atlassian fluency. A coaching practice that produces measurable results.

the_case.need: 7+ years SM / Agile delivery across multiple product engineering teams
the_case.answer: 10+ years. Eptura (~10 distributed teams, SaaS platform). A&E (~10 SVOD product workstreams). Paperless Post (2 concurrent product teams). Stealth AI startup (AI product squads). All commercial software.
the_case.detail: At Eptura I ran delivery across ~10 distributed engineering teams building a commercial SaaS platform. At A&E I managed ~10 concurrent product workstreams across Web, iOS, tvOS, Android, and Roku simultaneously. At Paperless Post I ran 2 concurrent product teams on the Flyer event-management platform. The pattern is consistent: multiple teams, product-focused organizations, commercial software that ships to real customers.

the_case.need: Deep Atlassian fluency — Jira workflows, boards, dashboards, and reporting
the_case.answer: Jira is the tool I configure and maintain, not just use. Custom workflows, fields, sprint boards, velocity dashboards, flow metrics, Jira Service Desk, Jira AI. I built the Jira infrastructure that gave Eptura leadership honest visibility into 10 teams.
the_case.detail: At Eptura I was the Jira configuration owner for the PMO — standardizing workflows, custom fields, and dashboards across ~10 teams. At A&E I built Jira from scratch for all teams (they were on Post-it notes when I arrived). At Crocs I maintained Azure DevOps and Jira boards throughout a complex release governance program. At Understood.org I administered Jira Service Desk to optimize QA workflows and automation. This is not casual Jira use — it is Jira administration at program scale.

the_case.need: Build and maintain delivery metrics (velocity, flow, predictability) for leadership visibility
the_case.answer: Eptura: built and published velocity, burndown, cycle time, and team health dashboards in ADO, Jira, and Smartsheet for ~10 teams. Crocs: real-time release readiness dashboards for leadership. The output: leadership that never had to ask for an update.
the_case.detail: I build metrics infrastructure that gives leadership a clear, honest view of team health and risk — not activity reports. At Eptura the dashboards I built became the standard for the entire PMO. At Crocs I built Release Radar, a real-time tracking framework that surfaced cross-environment dependencies before they threatened go-live. Metrics without action are just data — I use them to drive the coaching conversations that improve delivery.

the_case.need: Coach teams toward self-organization, healthy delivery practices, and continuous improvement
the_case.answer: 35%+ delivery efficiency gain at Eptura. 20% sprint carryover reduction. Agile adoption from scratch in Marketing (zero prior practice). Junior SM mentorship. That is the coaching track record.
the_case.detail: Coaching is not a workshop — it is sustained, individualized, and measured. At Eptura I coached ~10 teams through varying maturity levels over 18 months. I coached Product Owners on what makes a story executable. I ran retrospectives that produced actual workflow changes. I mentored junior Scrum Masters. The efficiency gains and carryover reduction did not happen from process enforcement — they happened from behavioral change driven by consistent coaching.

the_case.need: Partner with Product Owners to keep backlogs healthy, refined, and ready
the_case.answer: Deep PO partnership is the consistent pattern: Eptura (10 POs), Crocs (business owners + PO), Understood.org, A&E (product roadmap alignment), Paperless Post (backlog health and Optimizely-tied outcomes).
the_case.detail: I coach Product Owners on the difference between a story that is technically written and a story that is execution-ready. At Eptura I maintained DoR/DoD discipline across all teams and partnered with POs on backlog grooming and prioritization. At Paperless Post I aligned Jira tagging to Optimizely so backlog work tied directly to measurable product outcomes. PO partnership is not meeting attendance — it is making the backlog trustworthy.

the_case.need: Software-literate background — SDLC, CI/CD, release rhythms
the_case.answer: Front-end engineering background. Six production applications shipped this year via GitHub Actions CI/CD. Can read code, follow a system diagram, and engage substantively on a technical trade-off.
the_case.detail: I have been a front-end engineer (Organic Inc., Flavorpill), and I have shipped six production applications this year using Claude Code, GitHub Actions, React, TypeScript, Web Audio API, and Three.js/WebGL. The software-literate background is not theoretical — I built the CI/CD pipelines, debugged the edge cases, and pushed the production deployments. That means conversations with your engineering teams are grounded, not abstract.

the_stack.headline: The delivery ecosystem.
the_stack.subhead: The tools I build on, configure, and maintain — not just use.

nodes.label: Jira
nodes.desc: Workflows, custom fields, sprint boards, velocity dashboards, flow metrics, burndown, Jira Service Desk, Jira AI — configured and maintained at program scale
nodes.pm_role: Jira configuration owner for the PMO at Eptura (~10 teams). Built Jira from scratch at A&E (from Post-it notes). Used Jira AI for backlog synthesis and sprint summaries at Crocs.

nodes.label: Confluence
nodes.desc: Team documentation, working agreements, Definition of Done, retrospective-to-action tracking, process frameworks
nodes.pm_role: Standardized Jira/Confluence configuration across teams so delivery artifacts are findable, current, and trustworthy — not siloed per team.

nodes.label: Azure DevOps (ADO)
nodes.desc: Board management, sprint tracking, delivery health dashboards, release readiness reporting
nodes.pm_role: Maintained ADO delivery dashboards at Eptura and Crocs; used alongside Jira for cross-platform visibility.

nodes.label: Smartsheet
nodes.desc: Program-level reporting, capacity planning, leadership dashboards, roadmap tracking
nodes.pm_role: Built leadership-level delivery health reports at Eptura that synthesized across all ~10 teams into a single view.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions / CI/CD
nodes.desc: CI/CD pipelines for six production applications — build, test, deploy to GitHub Pages
nodes.pm_role: Hands-on CI/CD experience that makes release rhythm conversations with engineering teams credible. I know what a deployment pipeline looks like from the inside.

when_things_break.scenario: Sprint velocity drops 30% with no obvious cause — third consecutive sprint
response: Pull individual story completion data — is the drop uniform or concentrated in one team member or workstream?
Check if story size crept up without explicit sizing adjustment
1:1s with engineers before the retrospective — not after
Retrospective with explicit framing: 'Three sprints of this — what changed and what haven't we named yet?'
Jira flow metrics review: where in the workflow are stories stalling?

when_things_break.scenario: Cross-team dependency blocks two teams simultaneously mid-sprint
response: Convene an unscheduled sync immediately — not at the next Scrum-of-Scrums
Map the full dependency chain: who owns the blocker, what unblocks it, and how long?
Assess sprint goal impact across both teams — is a goal-preserving scope swap possible?
Escalate to leadership with a clear ask: decision needed, timeline, impact of delay
Update dependency tracking in Jira and communicate resolution status to all stakeholders

when_things_break.scenario: Product Owner is consistently unavailable for backlog refinement — stories entering sprint unready
response: Name the pattern directly in a 1:1 — not in a team ceremony
Quantify the cost: how many sprint stories have failed DoR in the last three sprints?
Propose a structural solution: time-boxed async refinement, delegated acceptance criteria, explicit DoR gate in sprint planning
Escalate to Product leadership if availability is structural, not personal
In the interim: enforce DoR at sprint planning — do not let unready stories into the sprint

how_i_run_a_room: Sprint ceremonies end with decisions made and action items owned — no deferred follow-ups
Retrospectives produce at least one measurable workflow change per sprint, tracked to resolution
Delivery metrics published before leadership asks for them — proactive, not reactive
Cross-team dependencies mapped and surfaced at Scrum-of-Scrums before they block delivery
Impediments have owners and resolution timelines within 24 hours of surfacing
Product Owner is a partner, not a meeting participant — I know what is in the backlog and why
Leadership updates are honest about risk — I do not smooth over problems

track_record.role: Senior Scrum Master / Agile Coach — Product PMO
points: ~10 distributed product engineering teams (India and California) building commercial SaaS
Jira configuration owner for the full PMO — workflows, dashboards, velocity and flow reporting
35%+ delivery efficiency gain; 20% sprint carryover reduction
Agile adoption from scratch in Marketing; junior SM mentorship org-wide
~10 concurrent SVOD product workstreams (Web, iOS, tvOS, Android, Roku)
Built Jira from scratch for all teams — workflows, boards, dashboards
Scrum-of-Scrums across ~6 teams; cultural shift from Post-it notes to structured delivery
Shipped all products against externally committed launch windows
2 concurrent product teams (iOS + mobile-web) on the Flyer event-management platform
Tied Jira backlog to Optimizely A/B testing outcomes — delivery connected to measurable product results
Delivered NEXUS Scrum coaching; standardized Jira/Confluence configuration across both teams
Release governance for a global publicly traded retailer — go/no-go, CAB approval, SOX-compliant
Built Release Radar framework giving leadership real-time delivery visibility
Jira AI embedded in delivery workflows; administrative overhead reduced ~30%
On-time go-live with clean post-launch stabilization

before: Sprint planning ends with commitments that teams don't believe in — but nobody said so
Cross-team dependencies are discovered after they've already blocked delivery
Leadership gets a dashboard that shows activity, not honest risk and health
Product Owner partnership is transactional — backlogs are written but not execution-ready
after: Sprint commitments are realistic, owned, and visible in Jira before planning ends
Dependencies are mapped and surfaced at Scrum-of-Scrums before they block anyone
Leadership gets velocity, flow, and predictability metrics with honest commentary on what they mean
PO partnership is continuous — I know what is coming in the next two sprints and why

the_ramp.deliverable: Jira audit across both teams — current workflow health, dashboard gaps, metric baselines. Meet every PO, tech lead, and team member. No changes yet; listen first.
Facilitate first full ceremony cycle. Identify top 3 impediments and accountability gaps per team. Propose first Jira improvement to Director, Product Operations.
Delivery metrics baseline published. First retrospective with measurable output. Cross-team dependency map complete. Direct coaching conversations underway where needed.
Running both teams cleanly. Leadership has honest visibility into health, predictability, and risk. No supervision needed — clear escalation paths established.
